skill.name: tdd-workflows-tdd-refactor
skill.description: Use when working with tdd workflows tdd refactor
skill.summary: Safely refactor code using Test-Driven Development principles with comprehensive test coverage
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 9 potential issues (external commands, weak crypto, reconnaissance patterns). All findings are FALSE POSITIVES - they occur in code documentation examples and markdown backticks, not executable code. No prompt injection attempts or malicious intent detected. This is a legitimate TDD refactoring skill.

low_findings.title: External Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: Ruby/shell backtick patterns detected at lines 23, 139, 159, 162. These are FALSE POSITIVES - the backticks contain code examples in TypeScript/JavaScript within markdown code blocks, not executable Ruby shell commands. The skill file is documentation-only with no executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern found in markdown code examples, not executable code. File is pure documentation with TypeScript examples in backticks for display purposes.

content.value_statement: Maintain code quality while refactoring without breaking existing functionality. This skill provides comprehensive TDD workflows to guide safe code transformations.
actual_capabilities: Guides systematic refactoring while keeping tests green
Detects and eliminates code smells using established patterns
Applies SOLID principles and design patterns appropriately
Performs incremental refactoring with safety verification
Provides before/after code examples for common refactoring techniques
Includes recovery protocols for failed refactoring attempts
limitations: Requires existing test coverage to refactor safely
Does not write new tests - focuses on refactoring existing code
Does not execute code directly - provides guidance and plans
Cannot guarantee performance improvements without profiling data
Relies on Claude Code Task tool for complex refactoring operations

use_cases.title: Legacy Code Modernization
use_cases.description: Refactor legacy codebases incrementally while maintaining functionality

use_cases.title: Code Quality Improvement
use_cases.description: Eliminate code smells and apply design patterns systematically

use_cases.title: Test-Supported Architecture Evolution
use_cases.description: Evolve architecture patterns safely using comprehensive test coverage

prompt_templates.title: Basic Refactoring Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this code while keeping all tests green. Focus on eliminating code smells and improving readability.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick refactoring of small code sections

prompt_templates.title: Pattern Application
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the Extract Method pattern to this code. Break down the long method into smaller, focused functions while maintaining all tests green.

prompt_templates.title: Comprehensive Refactoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this code using TDD refactor phase: establish green baseline, detect code smells, apply appropriate design patterns, verify with full test suite, and provide before/after metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Architecture Evolution
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this code to improve architecture using the Strangler Fig pattern. Gradually replace legacy components while maintaining test safety and provide incremental migration plan.

output_examples.input: Refactor this OrderProcessor class while keeping tests green
output_examples.output: Applied Extract Method, Value Objects, and Dependency Injection patterns. All tests passing. Created separate validation service, introduced OrderTotal value object, and injected dependencies. Code coverage maintained at 95%.

output_examples.input: Eliminate code smells in this 200-line function
output_examples.output: Decomposed into 8 focused methods using Extract Method pattern. Applied Single Responsibility Principle. Introduced Parameter Objects for complex method signatures. All 42 tests still passing. Cyclomatic complexity reduced from 45 to 8.

best_practices: Always run tests before refactoring to establish a green baseline
Make small, incremental changes and run tests after each modification
Commit after each successful refactoring to enable easy rollback
Separate refactoring changes from behavior changes completely
anti_patterns: Refactoring without test coverage - leads to undetected regressions
Making large-scale changes without intermediate test runs - high risk of breaking functionality
Mixing refactoring with feature additions - violates Single Responsibility Principle
Skipping rollback planning - makes recovery difficult when tests fail

faq.question: Do I need existing tests to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill requires existing test coverage to refactor safely. Without tests, refactoring risks breaking existing functionality. Consider writing characterization tests first for legacy code.

faq.question: How large should refactoring changes be?
faq.answer: Keep changes small and incremental. Run tests after each modification. Typical refactoring steps should change 5-20 lines of code and be immediately verifiable with your test suite.

faq.question: Can this skill write tests for my code?
faq.answer: No, this skill focuses on refactoring existing code while maintaining test coverage. It does not write new tests. For test generation, consider using a dedicated TDD test-writing skill.

faq.question: What if my tests fail during refactoring?
faq.answer: Immediately revert the last change, identify what broke, and apply smaller incremental changes. Use version control for safe experimentation. The skill includes recovery protocols for handling failures.

faq.question: Does this work with all programming languages?
faq.answer: The refactoring principles apply broadly, but code examples and patterns are primarily demonstrated in TypeScript. Concepts translate to Java, C#, Python, and other object-oriented languages.

faq.question: How do I know which design pattern to apply?
faq.answer: The skill provides guidance on pattern selection based on code smells detected. Start with the specific problem: duplicated code needs extraction, complex conditions need polymorphism, and tight coupling needs dependency injection.
